meta.welcomeSubtitle: When failure is not an option. Personalized safety simulations for the people who maintain CIRCOR's mission-critical pump and flow control operations.
meta.siteLabel: Warren Pumps — Pilot Site 1 of N

en.text: You are dispatched to perform routine overhaul on a Warren Pumps twin-screw fuel transfer pump returned from a Navy auxiliary vessel. The main electrical disconnect is locked out. As you approach the pump skid, you notice the auxiliary hydraulic control circuit is still active and the casing temperature gauge reads 68°C. The pump looks powered down. What do you do?

answers.text: Proceed — the main disconnect is locked and the pump is visually still.
answers.fb: Incorrect. Twin-screw pumps in marine fuel applications have multiple independent energy sources — electrical drive, hydraulic auxiliary control, residual fluid pressure in the casing, and thermal energy. A locked main disconnect does not isolate the hydraulic circuit, depressurize the casing, or eliminate burn risk from the 68°C casing.

answers.text: Stop. Isolate all four energy sources — electrical, hydraulic, residual fluid pressure, and verify thermal cooldown — before any disassembly.
answers.fb: Correct. Twin-screw pumps require multi-source LOTO. Each energy source must be individually controlled and verified before disassembly begins. The correct sequence: electrical first to prevent restart, hydraulic to release stored pressure, fluid pressure relief, then thermal verification that the casing is below safe touch temperature.

answers.text: Call your supervisor and wait for guidance before doing anything else.
answers.fb: Supervisor notification matters — but not before completing the lockout. You are already in the servicing position. Isolate the remaining energy sources first, then document the gap and notify your supervisor.

answers.text: Note the gap in the maintenance log and continue — you'll update the lockout list after the service is complete.
answers.fb: Proceeding with an incomplete LOTO puts you at direct risk of hydraulic burst, fluid burns from the 68°C casing, or entrapment in the screws if the auxiliary circuit re-energizes. Documentation comes after the hazard is controlled — not before.

en.text: Your LOTO is applied on the twin-screw pump. A contractor electrician arrives to assist with motor terminal disconnection. He looks at your locks already in place on the disconnect and asks: "Should I just sign your tag, or do I need my own lock?" What is the correct answer?

answers.text: Your locks already cover the system — he can begin work safely.
answers.fb: Incorrect. Under OSHA's multi-employer LOTO standard (29 CFR 1910.147), every authorized worker exposed to a hazardous energy source must apply their own personal lock. Your locks protect you. They do not protect him.

answers.text: He must apply his own personal lock to the disconnect, in addition to yours, before beginning work.
answers.fb: Correct. Multi-employer LOTO requires every exposed worker to apply their own lock — no exceptions, no shortcuts. The hasp accommodates multiple locks for exactly this reason. His lock is removed only by him when his work is complete.

answers.text: He should sign the LOTO log book confirming your locks are sufficient.
answers.fb: A signature in a log book does not protect a worker from re-energization. The lock — the physical device applied by the worker themselves — is the protection. A signature is supplementary documentation, not a substitute.

answers.text: You should remove your locks first, then he applies his own.
answers.fb: Removing your locks creates a window where the system has no protection at all. The correct sequence is additive — each worker adds their lock without removing any existing ones. Energy control is never sequential when multiple workers are exposed.

es.text: Está realizando el mantenimiento de rutina en una bomba de doble tornillo Warren Pumps. El desconector eléctrico principal está bloqueado. Al acercarse al equipo, nota que el circuito de control hidráulico auxiliar sigue activo y el indicador de temperatura de la carcasa marca 68°C. ¿Qué hace?
es.sub: Aislamiento de múltiples fuentes de energía.

answers.text: Continúas — el desconector principal está bloqueado y la bomba está visualmente quieta.
answers.fb: Incorrecto. Las bombas de doble tornillo tienen múltiples fuentes de energía independientes. Un desconector principal bloqueado no aísla el circuito hidráulico, no despresuriza la carcasa ni elimina el riesgo de quemaduras por los 68°C.

answers.text: Para. Aísla las cuatro fuentes — eléctrica, hidráulica, presión residual del fluido y verificación térmica — antes de cualquier desmontaje.
answers.fb: Correcto. Las bombas de doble tornillo requieren LOTO de múltiples fuentes. Cada fuente debe controlarse individualmente y verificarse antes del desmontaje.

answers.text: Llama a tu supervisor y espera instrucciones antes de hacer cualquier otra cosa.
answers.fb: Notificar al supervisor es importante — pero no antes de completar el bloqueo. Aísla las fuentes de energía restantes primero.

answers.text: Anótalo en el registro de mantenimiento y continúas.
answers.fb: Proceder con un LOTO incompleto te pone en riesgo directo. La documentación viene después de controlar el peligro.

es.text: Tienes tu LOTO aplicado. Un electricista contratista llega y pregunta: "¿Solo firmo tu etiqueta o necesito mi propio candado?" ¿Cuál es la respuesta correcta?

answers.text: Tus candados ya cubren el sistema — puede comenzar a trabajar de forma segura.
answers.fb: Incorrecto. Cada trabajador autorizado expuesto a una fuente de energía peligrosa debe aplicar su propio candado personal.

answers.text: Debe aplicar su propio candado personal al desconector, además de los tuyos, antes de comenzar.
answers.fb: Correcto. El LOTO multi-empleador exige que cada trabajador expuesto aplique su propio candado — sin excepciones.

answers.text: Debe firmar el libro de registro de LOTO confirmando que tus candados son suficientes.
answers.fb: Una firma no protege a un trabajador de la re-energización. El candado físico es la protección, no la documentación.

answers.text: Debes retirar tus candados primero y luego él aplica el suyo.
answers.fb: Retirar candados crea una ventana sin protección. La secuencia correcta es aditiva — nunca secuencial.

Maintenance Technician.pass: Strong response. As a maintenance technician on twin-screw pumps, this is the discipline that keeps you and your colleagues safe shift after shift.
Maintenance Technician.fail: Maintenance technicians face these decision points every shift. Every service is a fresh energy audit — no assumptions, no shortcuts.

Engineer.pass: As an engineer on twin-screw pump systems, you understand the risk profile. The challenge is ensuring this rigor translates consistently to every technician you work with.
Engineer.fail: Engineers often have the deepest system knowledge — and the highest risk of overconfidence. Familiarity with a machine is not the same as a completed energy audit.

Contractor.pass: Good response. Multi-employer LOTO is where most contractor-related incidents originate. You demonstrated you know the rule — your personal lock is required every time.
Contractor.fail: As a contractor, you operate under both your employer's LOTO program AND CIRCOR's site-specific procedures. Multi-employer LOTO is non-negotiable regardless of who is already locked out.

EHS Leader.pass: You already know the standard. The challenge is making sure every technician and contractor on your sites responds this consistently — especially under production pressure.
EHS Leader.fail: This scenario represents the exact decision point where recordable incidents happen. Your team needs repeated, realistic exposure to these choices before they become automatic.

Supervisor.pass: Good instinct. The challenge is consistency — making sure your entire team, including contractors, responds this way without you being present to reinforce it.
Supervisor.fail: Your team looks to you to model the right response. When supervisors tolerate incomplete LOTO or single-lock systems with multiple workers, it becomes the norm.

Maintenance Technician.pass: Buena respuesta. Esta disciplina es lo que mantiene a usted y a sus compañeros seguros turno tras turno.
Maintenance Technician.fail: Los técnicos se enfrentan a estos puntos de decisión en cada turno. Cada servicio requiere una auditoría de energía nueva — sin suposiciones.

Engineer.pass: Como ingeniero, conoce bien el sistema. El desafío es garantizar que este rigor se traduzca de manera consistente a cada técnico con quien trabaja.
Engineer.fail: Los ingenieros tienen el mayor riesgo de exceso de confianza. La familiaridad con una máquina no equivale a una auditoría de energía completa.

Contractor.pass: Buena respuesta. El LOTO multi-empleador es donde ocurren la mayoría de los incidentes de contratistas. Su candado personal es requerido cada vez.
Contractor.fail: Como contratista, opera bajo las reglas de su empleador Y las del sitio de CIRCOR. El LOTO multi-empleador no es negociable.

EHS Leader.pass: Ya conoce el estándar. El desafío es asegurarse de que cada técnico y contratista responda con esta consistencia bajo presión de producción.
EHS Leader.fail: Este escenario representa el punto de decisión exacto donde ocurren los incidentes registrables.

Supervisor.pass: Buen instinto. El desafío es la consistencia en todo su equipo, incluyendo contratistas, sin que usted esté presente.
Supervisor.fail: Su equipo lo observa para modelar la respuesta correcta. Cuando los supervisores toleran LOTO incompleto, se convierte en la norma.
